this is what you can build in a few hours:

- image generation pipelines – send a message to the coding agent and see it execute 20-30 complex steps
- self-updating admin panel – agent can update your UI on the fly
- document processing: your users can upload 50+ random files and ask agent to convert them
- video editing: ask it to crop, edit, convert videofiles with ffmpeg, using natural language
- data analysis: upload a big-ass CSV and ask Claude to analyze it

text:
When you were young, you fell in love. She was curious and interested, but it wasn’t reciprocal.

She was still exploring herself, so you went on a few dates, and  then randomly she cut you off. She wasn’t experienced yet, so she didn’t  say No explicitly. After all she enjoyed your attention.

But then you started noticing:

Once you were pushing her away, she would suddenly start paying  attention to you. And when you were getting too excited, she would lose  interest and ghost you.

And you learned: women like when you ignore them, women hate when you get too much into to them. Right? Wrong.

Fast forward a few years. You’re mature now. You’ve just met a  great girl. She’s also mature. And she’s The One. You love her and she  loves you.

And then, your training kicks in: as soon as you get too close to  her, you’re hearing the echoes of the past: “the closer you get to  women, the more of a pain expect it to be. it never ends well”. So you  put a wall between you both.

“Oh, I’m not THAT interested, what made you think it? you think i  LOVE you? girl, its been just a few dates, im not some naive BOY”.

She’s devastated. But she’s also mature, she has her own life, so  she gulps it in. Men aren’t gonna let her down, she has plenty of other  stuff to focus on. After all she likes you, so you keep going dating.

…and your brain goes “you see? it worked again. she LOVED it”. But  a small part of her died that day. A part you will never bring back.

Days go by. She got used to it. She’s not showered with love, but  at least she has a stable life. You keep holding yourself, trying to  pretend you don’t love her as much. And this goes for YEARS.

Until, one day, she’ll met a man who’s unapologetically open. Who  wears his heart on his chest, who’s not afraid of rejection, and who  tell her directly: i love you, i want to be with you, i will marry you,  and i will love you forever. And he means it.

She didn’t even know it was possible.

She will know that saying NO to this man is not an option. Not in  the sense that he will force her to marry him. But in the sense that he  will not STOP loving her. Cause his love is not a game. It’s not about  her. His love is his own, it exists as-is, and it doesn’t need any  approval or validation from anyone else.

Now, you, brother, have a choice. She will meet this man sometime  in her life, and since that day she’ll be with him forever.

This is not your choice. Your choice is whether tomorrow you will wake up to be that man, or if you let someone else have it.

text:
here's what yc actually wants to see in your AI coding transcripts (it's not what you think)

🧵🧵🧵

raw.full_text:
here's what yc actually wants to see in your AI coding transcripts (it's not what you think)

🧵🧵🧵

text:
in 2026, they're not checking if you can code -- they wanna see if you can THINK

anyone can prompt an AI to write code. what separates founders is knowing what to build and when to push back on the AI's output. they want to see:

raw.full_text:
in 2026, they're not checking if you can code -- they wanna see if you can THINK

anyone can prompt an AI to write code. what separates founders is knowing what to build and when to push back on the AI's output. they want to see:

text:
1. you're the driver, not the passenger — are you telling the AI what to build with clear intent, or are you going "make me an website" and reply "go" 5 times in a row?

2. velocity — how fast do you go from "i want X" to deployed thing? yc loves speed. a transcript where you ship a real feature in 30 min says more than a polished demo video

raw.full_text:
1. you're the driver, not the passenger — are you telling the AI what to build with clear intent, or are you going "make me an website" and reply "go" 5 times in a row?

2. velocity — how fast do you go from "i want X" to deployed thing? yc loves speed. a transcript where you ship a real feature in 30 min says more than a polished demo video

text:
3. debugging instinct — when shit breaks (and it will in any real transcript), do you flail or do you diagnose systematically?

4. product thinking mid-build — the best transcripts will show someone changing their mind mid-implementation because they realized the UX is wrong. you have to be actually following the output

raw.full_text:
3. debugging instinct — when shit breaks (and it will in any real transcript), do you flail or do you diagnose systematically?

4. product thinking mid-build — the best transcripts will show someone changing their mind mid-implementation because they realized the UX is wrong. you have to be actually following the output

text:
what they DON'T want to see:

- you asking the AI to explain basic concepts (signals you're learning on the job)
- accepting every suggestion without question (passenger mode)
- a perfectly clean transcript (means it's curated/fake)

raw.full_text:
what they DON'T want to see:

- you asking the AI to explain basic concepts (signals you're learning on the job)
- accepting every suggestion without question (passenger mode)
- a perfectly clean transcript (means it's curated/fake)

text:
5. slop detection — do you catch when the AI writes garbage? do you reject bad abstractions, over-engineering, or subtle bugs? this is the new literacy.

6. ambiguity handling — real features have ambiguous requirements. how you decompose "add payments" into actual steps reveals how you think.

raw.full_text:
5. slop detection — do you catch when the AI writes garbage? do you reject bad abstractions, over-engineering, or subtle bugs? this is the new literacy.

6. ambiguity handling — real features have ambiguous requirements. how you decompose "add payments" into actual steps reveals how you think.

text:
prompt:

⏺ battery optimization process

  1. audit — snapshot top cpu consumers, launch agents, sleep assertions
  2. triage — separate into: active drains (high cpu), idle bloat (loaded but 0%), hardware
  (battery health)
  3. kill the obvious — anything burning cpu that you don't use, just disable it
  4. investigate the rest — is 25% WindowServer a problem or expected? context matters (4k hdr =
   expected)
  5. leave what's harmless — 0% cpu idle processes aren't worth breaking
  6. monitor — sample over time to catch intermittent spikes

  the core insight: find cpu hogs, decide if they're necessary, kill the ones that aren't.
  that's it. everything else is noise.
